desc: write_log interpolates message directly into SQL, allowing SQL injection and causing valid messages containing apostrophes to fail.
read_logs concatenates the caller-supplied limit into SQL, allowing crafted values to alter or inject SQL.
write_log commits nondeterministically, so identical calls have inconsistent durability and may unexpectedly commit unrelated pending work.
read_logs uses a truthiness check for limit, so limit=0 incorrectly returns every row instead of no rows.
do_business_logic_but_sql_heavy silently suppresses all commit failures and returns results despite failed persistence.
Each setup call inserts another copy of the initialization records despite using CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, causing duplicate seed data.
The module-level connection and cursor create shared mutable state, are unsafe for concurrent callers, and remain unusable after main closes them.
main closes the cursor and connection only on normal completion, so any database or printing exception leaks both resources.
Random write counts, messages, limits, and commit behavior make business results irreproducible and difficult to test or reason about.
